facts_disclosed: Drives an old Land Rover Defender — 1998, held together by rust

turns.content: well that was a morning. spent forty minutes trying to get the defender started in minus three. just sat there turning the key like a fool while a farmer watched from his kitchen window.
That sounds miserable. Minus three and a stubborn engine is a rough combination. What was wrong with it -- battery struggling in the cold, or something else going on?

prob the glow plugs again. its a 98 so everything on it is original or held together with cable ties. more rust than actual metal at this point. farmer came out eventually and asked if I wanted a tow. very dignified.
A 1998 -- that's genuinely impressive that it's still running at all. Or, well, mostly running. Did the farmer end up towing you, or did it eventually turn over?

it caught on the maybe fifteenth try. made a noise like a dying animal which is ironic given my line of work. then I had to drive it another twenty miles to the next farm with the heater not working. couldnt feel my hands by the time I got there.

Driving twenty miles in minus three with no heater sounds genuinely dangerous, never mind uncomfortable. Have you thought about getting it properly looked at, or is it at the point where the repair costs would outweigh the value of the vehicle?

aye its been at that point for about four years tbh. the thing is nothing else gets through the tracks I have to drive. half the farms up here dont have proper roads. just mud and rocks and the odd river crossing. the defender handles all of it. when it starts.

That's the eternal Defender dilemma, isn't it? Nothing else goes where it goes, but nothing else breaks down as often either. The off-road capability is genuinely hard to replace. Do you have a backup plan for the days it completely refuses?

I phone the farmer and tell them ill be late. thats the backup plan. or if its really bad I borrow my neighbours wee suzuki jimny but trying to fit vet equipment in a jimny is like playing tetris with syringes.

Ha, the mental image of you cramming calving ropes and medicine boxes into a Jimny is something. At least the Jimny can handle rough terrain too, even if it's not exactly spacious. How often does the Defender leave you stranded like this?

once a month maybe. more in winter. the cold just kills it. and theres a leak somewhere that means the footwell fills up with water when it rains which up here is basically always. I keep wellies in the cab just to drive it.

Wellies to drive your own car -- that's a new one. A permanent puddle in the footwell can't be helping the rust situation either. It sounds like you're emotionally attached to this thing despite it actively trying to make your life harder.

wouldnt say emotionally attached. more like I know exactly how broken it is and a new vehicle would just be a different set of problems I havent learned yet. better the devil you know. also defenders hold their value weirdly well so selling it is always in the back of my mind but then id have to actually commit to something else.

That's a very pragmatic way to look at it. And you're right that older Defenders have become oddly collectible -- even the rusty ones fetch decent money. Though I'd imagine the 'wellies required' feature might knock a bit off the asking price. For now it sounds like you and the Defender are stuck with each other.
aye. stuck is the right word. it got me home today so it lives another week. if it doesnt start tomorrow though its getting pushed into a ditch.
Sounds like a fair deal -- daily reprieves based on performance. If it does end up in a ditch, at least it'll feel right at home there. Hope it starts for you tomorrow. Good luck with the cold.
